reasoning:
Looking at this problem through the lens of "desperacy" - I'm struck by how retirement systems are designed around the *absence* of desperation. They assume steady accumulation, avoiding crisis. But for gig workers, crisis IS the baseline. What if we designed FOR desperation rather than against it?

```json
{
  "label": "Desperation-Weighted Retirement",
  "design_principles": "Desperation is not a failure state to avoid—it's data to count. Income volatility becomes the asset, not the obstacle. The system thrives on inconsistency rather than fighting it.",
  "core_mechanism": "Months of financial desperation register as 'hardship equity' claims on a collective fund. Your struggles accumulate value. The more volatile your history, the larger your retirement stake.",
  "how_it_works": "1) Workers log earnings monthly to a Desperation Registry. Months below survival threshold generate Hardship Credits. 2) Gig platforms pay a Volatility Tax proportional to worker income variance. 3) During surge months (income >150% baseline), progressive auto-capture (5-30%) funds retirement. 4) At retirement, payout = (accumulated savings) × (1 + hardship_ratio). Someone who spent 40% of working months in desperation gets a 1.4x multiplier. 5) Hardship Credits are tradeable within volatility cohorts—workers can sell future surge obligations to cover current desperation.",
  "what_is_new": "Inverts the moral logic: struggling MORE yields MORE security. Desperation becomes bankable equity rather than shameful failure. Removes the requirement for consistency entirely.",
  "why_it_works": "Traditional systems punish volatility twice: once when you live it, again when you can't save through it. This system recognizes that surviving economic precarity IS participation worth compensating. It also solves the psychological barrier—workers stop avoiding the retirement system during hard times and actively engage with it.",
  "why_it_fails": "Moral hazard: workers might hide income to generate artificial hardship credits. Verification costs could be prohibitive. Political framing as 'rewarding failure' makes adoption difficult. Platforms will lobby fiercely against volatility taxes. Could entrench gig work rather than pressure employers toward stability.",
  "medium_term": "Gig platforms restructure to reduce worker volatility (lowering their tax). A new financial services sector emerges around hardship credit trading. Income reporting becomes normalized, creating unprecedented data on precarious work.",
  "long_term_vision": "Retirement security decouples from income consistency. A generation retires having never saved regularly but having accumulated hardship equity through documented economic struggle. The shame narrative around financial instability dissolves. Precarious work becomes a viable life path with genuine security, rather than a trap with no exit."
}
```

solution.label: Desperation-Weighted Retirement
solution.design_principles: Desperation is not a failure state to avoid—it's data to count. Income volatility becomes the asset, not the obstacle. The system thrives on inconsistency rather than fighting it.
solution.core_mechanism: Months of financial desperation register as 'hardship equity' claims on a collective fund. Your struggles accumulate value. The more volatile your history, the larger your retirement stake.
solution.how_it_works: 1) Workers log earnings monthly to a Desperation Registry. Months below survival threshold generate Hardship Credits. 2) Gig platforms pay a Volatility Tax proportional to worker income variance. 3) During surge months (income >150% baseline), progressive auto-capture (5-30%) funds retirement. 4) At retirement, payout = (accumulated savings) × (1 + hardship_ratio). Someone who spent 40% of working months in desperation gets a 1.4x multiplier. 5) Hardship Credits are tradeable within volatility cohorts—workers can sell future surge obligations to cover current desperation.
solution.what_is_new: Inverts the moral logic: struggling MORE yields MORE security. Desperation becomes bankable equity rather than shameful failure. Removes the requirement for consistency entirely.
solution.why_it_works: Traditional systems punish volatility twice: once when you live it, again when you can't save through it. This system recognizes that surviving economic precarity IS participation worth compensating. It also solves the psychological barrier—workers stop avoiding the retirement system during hard times and actively engage with it.
solution.why_it_fails: Moral hazard: workers might hide income to generate artificial hardship credits. Verification costs could be prohibitive. Political framing as 'rewarding failure' makes adoption difficult. Platforms will lobby fiercely against volatility taxes. Could entrench gig work rather than pressure employers toward stability.
solution.medium_term: Gig platforms restructure to reduce worker volatility (lowering their tax). A new financial services sector emerges around hardship credit trading. Income reporting becomes normalized, creating unprecedented data on precarious work.
solution.long_term_vision: Retirement security decouples from income consistency. A generation retires having never saved regularly but having accumulated hardship equity through documented economic struggle. The shame narrative around financial instability dissolves. Precarious work becomes a viable life path with genuine security, rather than a trap with no exit.
